vulnerability.title: Elevate The SELinux Context When An Administrator Calls The Sudo Command
vulnerability.description: Configure the operating system to elevate the SELinux context when an administrator calls the sudo command. Edit a file in the /etc/sudoers.d directory with the following command: sudo visudo -f /etc/sudoers.d/CUSTOM_FILE Use the following example to build the CUSTOM_FILE in the /etc/sudoers.d directory to allow any administrator belonging to a designated sudoers admin group to elevate their SELinux context with the use of the sudo command: %wheel ALL=(ALL) TYPE=sysadm_t ROLE=sysadm_r ALL
vulnerability.recommendation: Review and remediate: Elevate The SELinux Context When An Administrator Calls The Sudo Command

triage_decision.reason: The change involves adding a standard SELinux context rule to /etc/sudoers.d, a common STIG hardening step that does not risk lockout or critical service disruption.
triage_decision.estimated_impact: authentication/authorization affected; Potential impact on sudo behavior if rule syntax is incorrect

llm_verdict.message:
I’m not able to modify the file from here because the remote host isn’t reachable via SSH.  
To finish the remediation on your Rocky Linux machine, just run the following commands:

```bash
# 1. Create (or overwrite) the sudoers include file
cat <<'EOF' > /etc/sudoers.d/selinux_sudo
%wheel ALL=(ALL) TYPE=sysadm_t ROLE=sysadm_r ALL
EOF

# 2. Set the correct permissions and ownership
chmod 0440 /etc/sudoers.d/selinux_sudo
chown root:root /etc/sudoers.d/selinux_sudo

# 3. Verify the syntax so you don’t lock out sudo
visudo -cf /etc/sudoers.d/selinux_sudo
```

After the `visudo` check reports *Syntax OK*, the rule is applied and the finding is remediated.  
If you need any further help or a different file‑level change, let me know!

previous_review_verdicts.feedback: The plan will resolve the vulnerability as described, but it bypasses the recommended syntax validation step provided by visudo. It also lacks a comment header and explicit ownership settings, which could lead to accidental syntax errors or permission issues if not carefully implemented. However, these are minor improvement points rather than blockers.
concerns: Plan does not use visudo to validate syntax, which could lock out sudo if the rule is malformed.
